1:
The Chronicler opens his vast genealogy with the twelve sons of Israel, and the witnesses read the bare list as the seed-bed of everything that follows. The reformed John Gill notes first the name itself: these are the sons of Israel, or Jacob, who was given the name Israel for his power with God, and whose twelve sons are here set down — the first four according to their birth by Leah (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah), then the two sons of Zilpah, Leah's handmaid (Issachar and Zebulun), while between Dan and Naphtali, the sons of Bilhah, are placed Joseph and Benjamin, the sons of Rachel. Gill marks the studied arrangement of the roll, the handmaids' children ranged in among the free.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat the list as the foundation and starting-point for the tribal genealogies that follow. The enumeration of the families of Judah begins at v.3 and runs all the way to 1 Chronicles 4:23; the tribe issued from only three of Judah's five sons — Shelah, Pharez, and Zerah — yet subdivided into five great families, since Hezron and Hamul, the two sons of Pharez, founded houses of their own. They lay out the plan of the whole chapter in advance: from Zerah only the names of a few famous men (vv.6-8); then the three branches of Hezron (v.9) — the line of Ram down to David, the house of Caleb, and the house of Jerahmeel.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the list into doctrine. The twelve sons are that illustrious number celebrated almost throughout the whole Bible; and though the personal character of several was none of the best — the first four much blemished — yet the covenant was entailed on their seed, for it was of grace, free grace, that it was said, Jacob have I loved, not of works, lest any man should boast. Judah's tribe was the most praised, increased, and dignified, and so its genealogy stands first and largest, the illustrious tree of which Christ was to be the top branch. In its first branches Henry meets some that were very bad (Er, Onan, and the incest of Tamar), some very wise and good (Heman, Ethan, Calcol, and Dara), and some very great (Nahshon and Salmon) — proof, he says, that the best and most honourable families may have those belonging to them that are blemishes. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke refers the reader to the parallel rolls, the births recorded from Genesis 29:32 onward and the household that went down into Egypt in Genesis 46:8.
3:
"The sons of Judah; Er, and Onan, and Shelah" — the register begins, and the first question the witnesses raise is why Judah, only the fourth-born of Jacob, should head the whole genealogy. The reformed John Gill answers with Kimchi that the book treats chiefly of the kings of Judah, but presses a deeper reason: from Judah sprang not only Jesse and David but the King Messiah himself. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees on both counts — Judah is put first because the best part of the birthright, the dominion, was conferred on him in Jacob's blessing (Genesis 49:8), and because the Messiah was to come out of his loins.

The grammatical-historical voices fasten instead on the shadow that falls across the very first name. Keil and Delitzsch note that the five sons of Judah are given exactly as in Genesis 46:12, and that the remark on Er is quoted word for word from Genesis 38:7. Charles Ellicott makes that same observation the key to the Chronicler's whole method: suppressing every other detail about Judah's sons, the writer copies this one statement — that Er, the firstborn, was, or became, or proved, evil in the sight of the LORD, and that the LORD slew him — intact from Genesis, because it thoroughly harmonises with the moral he wishes drawn from the entire history of his people. Ellicott adds that the mother of these three was the daughter of Shua the Canaanitess, Judah's wife.

So the witnesses hold two truths in tension over this opening verse. On the one hand Judah is exalted: his is the sceptre-tribe, the tribe of David and of Christ, and therefore his genealogy comes first and runs longest. On the other hand the line opens not with glory but with a death under divine judgment — Er cut off for his wickedness, the Canaanite marriage in the background, the incest of Tamar soon to follow. The Chronicler, the witnesses agree, does not sanitise the ancestry of the Messiah; he lets the reader see at the outset that the royal seed came through a house carrying its own sins, so that the honour of the line is owed not to the purity of the fathers but to the electing grace that chose Judah for the promise.
4:
"And Tamar his daughter in law bare him Pharez and Zerah; all the sons of Judah were five." The witnesses do not look away from the scandal the verse records. The reformed John Gill states it plainly: these twins were born through incest, Judah's own union with his daughter-in-law, and with them the sons of Judah numbered five in all. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott points the reader to the full narrative in Genesis 38:8, where the whole story of Tamar and the birth of the twins is told, and he notes the spellings the tradition carried: Pharez is the Hebrew Perez, and Zerah appears as Zarah in Genesis 38:30 and as Zara in the Lord's own genealogy at Matthew 1:3.

Keil and Delitzsch gather the branch that will matter. The two sons of Pharez, Hezron and Hamul, are named according to Genesis 46:12 and the census of Numbers 26:21; and it is through Pharez, and through Hezron in particular, that the whole line to David will presently be traced. Zerah, the other twin, will be dismissed after a few remembered names. Thus the verse quietly sets the two brothers on their divergent paths — the one to carry the royal seed, the other to fade into a handful of famous men.

What most arrests the witnesses is that the Messiah's pedigree should run straight through so dark a door. Tamar's name stands here, as it stands in Matthew 1:3, among the mothers named in the genealogy of the Christ; the incest is not hidden but recorded, the twins numbered among the five sons of Judah without apology. The lesson the expositors draw, in harmony with the moral Ellicott found in the verse before, is that the line of promise was preserved through human sin rather than by human merit — Pharez, born of a shameful union, taking his place as an ancestor of the throne of David and of the birth at Bethlehem. The grace that chose this line, the witnesses imply, is the same grace that would receive sinners into the kingdom the line was appointed to bring.
5:
"The sons of Pharez; Hezron, and Hamul." The verse is a single quiet link, yet the witnesses recognise it as the hinge on which the whole royal genealogy turns. The reformed John Gill notes simply that Pharez was one of the twins born to Judah, and that here his two sons are named. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the parallels that give the verse its weight: the two sons are taken from Genesis 46:12, which also names the five sons of Judah, and the census of Numbers 26:21 registers the clans that sprang from them — the Hezronites and the Hamulites — as counted in the mustering held by Moses in the wilderness.

The point the witnesses draw out is that these two names are not incidental but foundational. Of the two sons of Pharez it is Hezron who carries the promise; from him the record will trace three great branches (v.9), and through one of them the descent to David and to the Christ. Hamul, by contrast, founds a clan that is counted in the census and then heard of no more in this chapter. So the verse silently divides the way: the line of promise runs on through Hezron, while Hamul's house takes its place among the ordinary families of Judah.

Ellicott's appeal to the wilderness census underscores what the genealogy quietly assumes — that these were not mere individuals but the fathers of enduring clans, whose names were still borne by living families generations later when Moses numbered the tribes on the plains of Moab, the Hezronites and Hamulites counted side by side. The Chronicler, writing after the exile, reaches back through that census and through Genesis to anchor the restored community of Judah in the same two houses of Pharez, that they might know themselves the true continuation of the ancient tribe. The line that would produce a king and a Redeemer is here reduced to two names; but the witnesses see in that very economy the steady, hidden faithfulness of God, preserving through obscure and unrecorded generations the seed he had marked out for the throne, so that not a link of the chain toward David was ever lost.
6:
"And the sons of Zerah; Zimri, and Ethan, and Heman, and Calcol, and Dara: five of them in all." Before the record leaves the second twin, it pauses on a cluster of famous names, and the witnesses gather around two questions of identity. The reformed John Gill marks only that Zerah was the other twin of Judah, but the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke passes on the Targum's high estimate of these five: "These were all chief men; and on them the spirit of prophecy rested."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott treats the list critically. The first name, Zimri, he judges a merely accidental variant of Zabdi — both genuine Hebrew names, and since Zimri here and Zabdi at Joshua 7:1 are alike called sons of Zerah, their identity seems proved, the more so as m is easily confused with b, and d with r. The remaining four he identifies with the sages of 1 Kings 4:31, where Solomon is said to be wiser than "Ethan the Ezrahite, and Heman, and Chalcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol." The first three names coincide, Dara differs from Darda by a single letter, and many manuscripts of Chronicles with the Syriac, Arabic, and Targum actually read Darda. Ethan is there called an Ezrahite, but Ezrah and Zerah are equivalent forms, and the Vatican Greek even calls him a Zarhite (Numbers 26:13). As for "sons of Mahol," Ellicott takes Mahol in its usual sense of the sacred dance (Psalms 149:3; Psalms 150:4), so that the four were "sons of dancing," that is, sacred musicians, likely adopted into the Levitical guilds where such music was hereditary — though whether Ethan and Heman are the temple singers of 1 Chronicles 6:33 he leaves open.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reaches the same conclusion more cautiously: if these are the sons of Mahol of 1 Kings 4:31, then either the same man bore two names, Zerah and Mahol, as was usual among the Hebrews, or one was their immediate father and the other their grandfather. They are named here, he says, because they were the glory of their father's house; when the Holy Ghost would magnify Solomon's wisdom, he declared him wiser than these four — and that four brothers should be so eminent was a rare thing. So the witnesses converge: the obscure twin Zerah, dismissed from the royal line, is yet remembered for a household of proverbial wise men.
7:
"And the sons of Carmi; Achar, the troubler of Israel, who transgressed in the thing accursed." Out of the whole line of Zerah the record singles out one grandson, and it is not a hero but a byword. The reformed John Gill untangles the compressed pedigree: the Targum adds "this is Zimri," and since Carmi is elsewhere called the son of Zabdi, who seems to be the same as Zimri, some read "the sons of Zimri, Carmi, Achar," making Achar the grandson of Zimri. His proper name, Gill notes with Jarchi, was Achan, but he is called Achar here by way of reproach.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the name-play at the centre. At Joshua 7:1 the man is "Achan, son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah," so that the family of Carmi were Zarhites; yet Joshua 7:24 calls him simply "Achan the son of Zerah" — proof, Ellicott says, that we must be cautious of pressing such phrases literally, since "son of" may leap over generations. The Hebrew rings a bitter pun, Achar 'ocher Yisrael, "Achar, the troubler of Israel"; Joshua's question, "Why hast thou troubled us?" and the naming of the valley of Achor, "trouble," play on the same root (Joshua 7:25). Probably, he concludes, Achan is an old error for Achar — or rather the Chronicler has deliberately fixed the reproachful form. Keil and Delitzsch agree that the one thing recorded of him is that he transgressed in the devoted spoil, and note that of the whole line of Zerah only these famous, or infamous, men are set down before the record returns to Hezron.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws both threads together: Carmi is mentioned because he was the son of Zimri, also called Zabdi (Joshua 7:1), and the man is called Achan there but Achar here, with a little variation for greater significance, for Achar signifies a troubler. The witnesses thus make this single verse a small sermon: the Chronicler will not let the reader forget that the one grandson of Zerah worth naming is the man whose sin at Jericho brought trouble on all Israel, his very name turned into a monument of his transgression.
8:
"And the sons of Ethan; Azariah." A single name closes the branch of Zerah, and the witnesses admit at once how little can be said of it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott states flatly that nothing is known of this Ethanite Azariah; the writer plainly wished to name only the historically famous members of the Zarhite branch of Judah — in v.6 the four proverbial sages, in v.7 Achar who brought woe upon Israel by taking of the devoted spoil — and here one more remembered name before he turns away.

The reformed John Gill weighs why the rest of Zerah's posterity is passed over in silence. Either, with Kimchi, the writer could not find their genealogy; or, as Gill himself prefers, he cuts short the line of Zerah on purpose, because the kingdom did not proceed from him, and hastens back to the genealogy of Hezron, from which it did; or perhaps the other sons simply had no children to record. Whichever it was, the effect is the same: Zerah is given his handful of famous names and then dismissed.

The witnesses find in this deliberate brevity a clue to the Chronicler's mind. He is not compiling an exhaustive family tree but writing a history bent toward the throne of David. Zerah's house yields four proverbial sages and a notorious troubler, and Ethan's yields one otherwise unknown Azariah — a name that recurs often enough elsewhere, Ellicott remarks, but of this bearer of it nothing at all survives — and with that the record has said all it means to say of the elder twin's descendants. Gill's three reasons cover the ground between them: the fuller genealogy may simply have perished with time; or the writer, knowing the royal line did not spring from Zerah, chose to break it off and return to Hezron; or the remaining sons of Zerah may have died childless, leaving nothing to trace. The whole weight of the genealogy now shifts to Pharez, and within Pharez to Hezron, so that the reader feels the narrowing of the stream: from the twelve tribes to Judah, from Judah's five sons to Pharez, and now from Pharez's brother Zerah back to Pharez's son Hezron, along whose channel alone the promise runs on toward the king who is to come.
9:
"The sons also of Hezron, that were born unto him; Jerahmeel, and Ram, and Chelubai." With this verse the genealogy returns to its main channel, and the witnesses treat the three names as the source of the three great streams that fill the rest of the chapter. The reformed John Gill notes only where they were born — the Targum says in Timnath, but most likely, he thinks, in Egypt, during the sojourn of Jacob's house.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the architecture. From Hezron's three sons branch three lines, though the record enumerates them in an order of significance rather than of birth: first the family of Ram (vv.10-17), because King David descended from him; then the family of Chelubai, or Caleb (vv.18-24), from whose line came the illustrious Bezaleel; and last the posterity of the firstborn, Jerahmeel (vv.25-41). They observe too the Hebrew idiom, "what was born to him," a passive standing impersonally for "to whom one bore," and note that Ram is called Aram in the Lord's genealogy at Matthew 1:3, while Chelubai reappears simply as Caleb (v.18).

Charles Ellicott adds the sense of the names and a caution about their nature. Jerahmeel means "God pitieth"; Ram, called Aram in Matthew 1, is a synonym of that name, both meaning "high" and used interchangeably (compare Ram in Job 32:2 with Aram in Genesis 22:21); and Chelubai is strictly "the Chelubite," a gentilic form built from Caleb (v.18). This last, Ellicott urges, shows that we are dealing here not so much with individual sons of Hezron as with whole families or clans of Hezronites. The witnesses do not divide but complement one another: three brothers, or three houses, born to Hezron, from whom the Chronicler will draw the royal line of David, the artificer of the tabernacle, and the clans of the southern desert. That the record should set Ram in the middle by birth and yet trace his line first, out of order, is itself eloquent to the witnesses; the arrangement is governed not by seniority but by David, toward whom the whole book leans, so that the three names of this single verse already contain in miniature the framework of everything still to come.
10:
"And Ram begat Amminadab; and Amminadab begat Nahshon, prince of the children of Judah." The line now runs straight for David, and the witnesses read it against both its parallels and its silent gaps. The reformed John Gill observes that Ram is the same as Aram, and that the descent is carried down from him to Jesse in the very same order as elsewhere; only here is Nahshon, the son of Amminadab, given his title as prince of the children of Judah.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch develop both points. This genealogy of Ram's family, traced through six members down to Jesse, is also found in Ruth 4:19; but only here is Nahshon lifted above the rest by the addition "prince of the sons of Judah." Nahshon was indeed the prince of Judah at the Exodus, who led the tribe in the wilderness and offered first among the tribal princes (Numbers and the mustering under Moses). And here Keil and Delitzsch press the great chronological difficulty of the whole line: between Nahshon, a contemporary of Moses, and Pharez, who was about fifteen when Jacob's house went down into Egypt, lie the four hundred and thirty years of the sojourn, and yet only three names — Hezron, Ram, and Amminadab — are given to fill them, so that several links must plainly have been passed over.

The same gap, they add, yawns on the other side: from Nahshon to David is more than four hundred years, and the four generations of Salma, Boaz, Obed, and Jesse are far too few to span it, so that here also the less famous ancestors of David have been omitted. This candid reckoning is, for the witnesses, no embarrassment but an insight into how Hebrew genealogy works — not as an unbroken census of every father and son, but as a selective chain that links the great names and leaves the obscure generations unnamed. The line to David is true, but it is a summary of a longer descent, its length telescoped so that the reader may see the promise running unbroken from Judah through Nahshon the prince to the shepherd-king.
13:
"And Jesse begat his firstborn Eliab, and Abinadab the second, and Shimma the third, Nethaneel the fourth, Raddai the fifth, Ozem the sixth, David the seventh." The whole genealogy has bent toward this verse, yet it opens a puzzle the witnesses cannot pass by: here David is the seventh son, but 1 Samuel 16:10 shows Jesse had eight. The reformed John Gill lays out the explanations. One of the eight is not reckoned here, either — with Aben Ezra and Kimchi — because he was by another mother, and the writer names only those of the same mother as David; or because he died before David came to the kingdom; or, on a Midrash Kimchi cites, his name was Elihu and he was younger than David (1 Chronicles 27:18). Jarchi's word is the tenderest: the writer, having found the pearl — David — reckons not the eighth son at all. Gill notes that the Syriac and Arabic versions do insert him, reading "Elihu the seventh, David the eighth," while some suppose the missing eighth to be not a son but a grandson of Jesse, Jonathan the son of Shimea, the third son here called Shimma.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley touches the same difficulty from the side of Eliab, the firstborn: he is called also Elihu in 1 Chronicles 27:18 — unless that Elihu was another person, and the word "brother" there is taken more largely for a kinsman.

Beneath the arithmetic the witnesses feel the theological point. Seven sons stand before Jesse, and the seventh, the youngest named, is the one God chose — the shepherd passed over by men and singled out by heaven. The very confusion over the number, whether David was seventh or eighth, only underlines how little the son of Jesse was expected to matter; the record must almost be corrected to make room for him. And yet it is his name that crowns the list, the point toward which Judah, Pharez, Hezron, Ram, and Boaz have all been tending. The blemished and obscure line of the sons of Jesse yields at last the pearl, and the genealogy has reached the man for whose sake it was written.
16:
"Whose sisters were Zeruiah, and Abigail." The genealogy pauses to name two women, and the witnesses agree it does so for the sake of their sons. The reformed John Gill notes simply that Zeruiah and Abigail were sisters of David. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain why they are here at all: David's sisters have become known through their heroic sons. Zeruiah is the mother of the three great warriors of the Davidic history — Abishai, Joab, and Asahel (2 Samuel 2:18) — whose father is nowhere named, "because their more famous mother challenged the greater attention." Abigail, they add, was according to 2 Samuel 17:25 the daughter of Nahash, and so, strictly, only a half-sister of David; she was the mother of Amasa, the captain of the host, notorious for his part in Absalom's conspiracy.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already drawn the family together in his survey of the chapter: David's three great commanders, Joab, Abishai, and Asahel, were the sons of one of his sisters, and Amasa of another; and three of these four went down slain to the pit, though they were the terror of the mighty. The verse thus opens, in two names, upon the whole military history of the reign — the sons of Zeruiah who served and troubled David by turns, and the son of Abigail who died by Joab's treacherous hand.

A small point of relationship draws the witnesses' care. That Abigail is called daughter of Nahash in Samuel, while these sisters are reckoned among the children of Jesse, leads Keil and Delitzsch to make her a half-sister — whether by an earlier husband of Jesse's wife, or by some other tie the record does not spell out. The Chronicler, characteristically, states only the bare fact of the kinship and leaves the reader to fill it from the histories. What he wishes noted is that the mightiest men of David's wars were the king's own nephews, bone of his bone; the throne he was raised to was defended, and sometimes endangered, by the sons of his sisters, so that even this brief genealogical aside is heavy with the coming story.
17:
"And Abigail bare Amasa: and the father of Amasa was Jether the Ishmeelite." The witnesses turn from the sisters to the tangled parentage of Amasa, and here a genuine difficulty surfaces. The reformed John Gill first fixes the man: Amasa was Absalom's general, afterwards reconciled to David and marked out to be captain of his host, but slain by Joab (2 Samuel 20:10) before he could hold the post. His father is here called Jether the Ishmeelite — yet at 2 Samuel 17:25 the same man is named Ithra an Israelite.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley meets the clash head-on: Jether was an Ishmaelite by birth or by habitation, but by profession an Israelite, a proselyte reckoned to the covenant people, so that both notices are true, each looking at him from a different side. Clarke, of the same school, passes on the Targum's colourful gloss on the name: they called him Jether, it says, "because he girded himself with his sword," that he might help David with the Arabians when Abner sought to destroy David and the whole race of Jesse as unfit to enter the congregation of the LORD on account of Ruth the Moabitess. The tradition is fanciful, but it preserves a memory that this father of a Hebrew captain stood somehow between Israel and the desert peoples.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, simply set the fact in its place: Abigail, David's (half-)sister, bore Amasa to Jether the Ishmeelite, and so the captain who would lead Absalom's rebel army was, like Joab and Abishai, of the king's own kindred. The witnesses converge on a quiet irony the verse contains. The blood of Israel and of Ishmael, of the covenant line and of a proselyte from among the outsiders, met in Amasa; and the discrepancy of "Ishmeelite" and "Israelite" is not a contradiction to be feared but a window, showing how the house of Jesse had drawn in a stranger, and how the wars of David would be fought among cousins whose very ancestry crossed the old boundary between Abraham's chosen and his rejected sons. What Gill, Wesley, Clarke, and Keil and Delitzsch together make of the verse is that the Chronicler records the mixed parentage without embarrassment, content to let the reconciliation of the two names rest on the simple fact of the man's incorporation into Israel.
18:
"And Caleb the son of Hezron begat children of Azubah his wife, and of Jerioth." With this verse the record leaves the royal line to trace the house of Caleb, and the witnesses must first clear away two confusions. The reformed John Gill insists that this Caleb, the son of Hezron, is the same as Chelubai (v.9), and is not to be mistaken for the famous Caleb the son of Jephunneh of the books of Numbers, Joshua, and Judges. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the whole Calebite section, marvels that the tribe of Judah kept its genealogies so full and exact when the other tribes did not — a special providence, he says, for the clearing of the genealogy of Christ — and notes that from this very house came Bezaleel, the head-workman who built the tabernacle (v.20).

The verse itself, however, is textually knotted, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays the tangle bare. The Hebrew as it stands does not plainly say Caleb begat children "of Azubah his wife"; read flatly, it seems to say he begat "Azubah a woman, and Jerioth," with "these are her sons" following. But since v.19 goes on, "when Azubah was dead, Caleb took Ephrath," Azubah must have been a wife, not a daughter. Ellicott judges the text corrupt — the Septuagint and Syriac already diverge — and would read "his wife, the daughter of Jerioth," rendering: "Caleb begat sons with Azubah the daughter of Jerioth."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes the plainer path: Azubah is called "his wife" by way of distinction, while Jerioth was probably only a concubine, and a barren one, so that on Azubah's death Caleb married again. Clarke adds the Targum's fancy that Azubah ("forsaken") was so named because barren and despised, until she was comforted and adorned with wisdom and spun goats' hair for the tabernacle court. Henry, characteristically, mines the whole section for comfort — here are men who died childless, one who had only a daughter, families that terminate not in a person but in a place — and bids the childless not think their case strange, for God orders these affairs by an incontestable sovereignty and will be himself better than ten sons to those who love him.
19:
"And when Azubah was dead, Caleb took unto him Ephrath, which bare him Hur." The witnesses read the verse both for the name of the new wife and for the famous line that springs from her. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that Ephrath appears in the lengthened form Ephratah at v.50 and again in 1 Chronicles 4:4; and from her son Hur descended, through Uri, the illustrious Bezaleel, the skilful architect of the tabernacle (v.20). Charles Ellicott adds the geography hidden in the name: Ephrath is the ancient name by which Bethlehem was called (Micah 5:2), so that Caleb's second marriage plants his house in the very town where David and, long after, the Christ would be born.

The reformed John Gill records a curious tradition and its correction. The Targum identifies Ephrath with Miriam, "who was called Ephrath" — making Caleb's wife the sister of Moses; but, Gill objects, according to Josephus it was not Caleb but his son Hur who was the husband of Miriam the sister of Moses. The legend, whatever its worth, at least shows how early readers strained to link this Calebite house with the great names of the Exodus; and Gill's careful correction — that if any of Caleb's family married Miriam it was Hur, not Caleb himself — guards the genealogy from being bent to serve the tradition.

The witnesses find the true significance not in the legend but in the plain descent. A wife dies, a second is taken, a son named Hur is born — and from that ordinary sequence of marriages and births comes the man whose hands would fashion the ark, the table, and the golden lampstand of the sanctuary. Ephrath's name binds the house to Bethlehem, and Bethlehem binds it, in the reader's memory, to the royal and messianic hope; so that even this Calebite branch, which is not the line of David, is woven into the same sacred geography. The Chronicler records the marriage soberly, but the witnesses hear in "Ephrath, which bare him Hur" the quiet convergence of the tabernacle and the town of the King.
20:
"And Hur begat Uri, and Uri begat Bezaleel." Three generations bring the Calebite line to its most celebrated member, and the witnesses weigh whether he is indeed the man of the Exodus. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke has no doubt: this was probably the famous artist named in Exodus 31:2 — the Bezaleel whom God filled with wisdom to devise all the cunning work of the tabernacle. The grammatical-historical voices agree. Keil and Delitzsch identify him with the skilful architect of the tabernacle, and Charles Ellicott points to the express words that describe "Bezaleel, son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah," divinely qualified for building the Tent of Meeting — the same three names, in the same order, as stand here.

The reformed John Gill alone hesitates. This Bezaleel, he thinks, is not to be reckoned the same as the tabernacle-builder, even though the fathers and grandfathers of both bear the same names and both belong to the tribe of Judah — a caution born of the chronological distance the genealogy compresses, for the Exodus artificer lived generations before the Chronicler's usual reckoning would place this Bezaleel. So a small debate stands: the weight of the witnesses takes the coincidence of name, father, grandfather, and tribe as proof of identity, while Gill treats it as a striking likeness only.

Ellicott offers a reconciling insight that eases the difficulty. Bezaleel, he grants, is no doubt a real person; but Hur is probably a Calebite clan, established at "Ephrath, which is Bethlehem" (Genesis 35:19), so that "Hur begat Uri" may name a house rather than a single father. Read so, the genealogy telescopes clan and man together, and the artificer of the sanctuary can stand at its end without the reckoning of years forbidding it — the same principle by which Keil and Delitzsch had already accounted for the missing links between Pharez and David. The witnesses thus leave the reader with a Judahite of Caleb's house whose gifted hands built the dwelling-place of God — the same tribe that would give Israel its king now giving it the maker of its holy things, so that both the throne and the tabernacle are shown springing from the sons of Judah.
21:
"And afterward Hezron went in to the daughter of Machir the father of Gilead, whom he married when he was threescore years old; and she bare him Segub." The verse marries a man of Judah into the house of Manasseh, and the witnesses read it on two levels at once. The reformed John Gill takes it as plain history: Machir was the son of Manasseh and Gilead his grandson; the wife's name is not given, though Gill guesses it was Abiah. He passes on the Targum's harsher reading — "he enticed a virgin, the daughter of Machir" — which hints at fornication before the marriage, and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley notes that Gilead himself was a man of noted valour, the great champion of those parts.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch insist on the literal sense of the titles. This marriage came after the birth of the sons of v.9, when Hezron was sixty; and Machir is called "father of Gilead" not as founder of the region but in the proper sense, for Machir, the firstborn of Manasseh (Numbers 26:29), begot a literal son named Gilead, as the genealogy of Zelophehad's daughters (Numbers 27:1) makes clear. Segub is the fruit of the union.

Charles Ellicott hears something more in the same words. Machir was the firstborn of Manasseh, to whom Moses gave the land of Gilead (Numbers 32:40; Deuteronomy 3:15), and the great clan of Machir was the ruling clan there; so it is possible, he suggests, to see in "Hezron married the daughter of Machir" a metaphorical statement that a branch of the Hezronites amalgamated with the Machirites of Gilead, the "daughter of Machir" standing for the clan, as Scripture speaks of the "daughter of Zion" (Isaiah 37:22) or the "daughter of Babylon" (Isaiah 47:1). Keil and Delitzsch add that Machir is deliberately marked off as "father of Gilead" to distinguish him from other men of the same name, and that the great age of Hezron at this marriage is itself the reason the record pauses over it. So the witnesses divide gently over whether the verse tells of a single late marriage or the fusion of two clans — but agree that here a house of Judah struck root in the trans-Jordan land of Manasseh, and that the union of the tribes it records, whether by wedlock or by settlement, was owned and remembered by the genealogists of both.
22:
"And Segub begat Jair, who had three and twenty cities in the land of Gilead." The son of that Judah-Manasseh marriage becomes a lord of the eastern hill-country, and the witnesses trace both the man and the puzzle of his tribe. The reformed John Gill notes the twenty-three cities and weighs how Jair came by them: Kimchi thought he inherited them in right of his wife, but Gill judges it more likely they were what he took by force of arms from the former inhabitants.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the Pentateuch's notices of these towns, the Havoth-jair or "tent-villages of Jair." At Numbers 32:40 the Manassite clan of the sons of Machir took Gilead from the Amorites, Moses assigned it to Machir, and Jair took their tent-villages and called them by his own name; the same is told at Deuteronomy 3:14. Jair, Ellicott concludes, is really the name of a group of kindred families or clans, settled in the twenty-three cities — not merely one man but a house. He even sketches the double descent in a little chart, joining Judah and Manasseh in a single stem: Pharez, Hezron, then Hezron's marriage to the daughter of Machir, then Segub, then Jair — so that the tent-villages of the east were held by a family sprung from the union of the two tribes.

What most engages the witnesses is the crossing of tribal lines the verse implies. Keil and Delitzsch observe that this Jair, a descendant of Hezron of Judah on his father's side, is on his mother's side of the tribe of Manasseh, and is accordingly set down in Numbers and Deuteronomy as a Manassite. So the genealogy quietly records a man reckoned to two tribes at once — born of Judah through Segub, counted with Manasseh in the conquest of Gilead. The Chronicler, who cares above all for Judah, nonetheless follows this Judahite branch out into the east and lets it be numbered with another tribe, a reminder that the twelve houses of Israel were bound together by marriage and inheritance far more intricately than the tidy tribal lists suggest. Jair's twenty-three cities stand as a monument to a family that belonged, in a sense, to both sides of the Jordan.
23:
"And he took Geshur, and Aram, with the towns of Jair, from them, with Kenath, and the towns thereof, even threescore cities. All these belonged to the sons of Machir the father of Gilead." The verse is a thicket of names and pronouns, and the witnesses part company over who did the taking. The Authorized reading, which the reformed John Gill follows, makes Jair the conqueror — he took Geshur and Aram with the towns; yet Gill also cites the Targum's opposite sense, that "the Geshurites and Aramaeans took the villages of Jair from them," that is, from Jair's descendants in later times.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott urges that opposite reading as the true one: rather, "Geshur and Aram took the Havoth-jair from them — Kenath and her daughters, sixty cities." Geshur and Aram he identifies as the Aramean state of Geshur, north-west of Bashan near Hermon and the Jordan, an independent kingdom in David's day (2 Samuel 3:3); the particle before Kenath may be the sign of the object or the preposition "with," and Kenath itself he locates at the modern Kanawat on the slope of the Hauran, the town that Numbers 32:41 says Nobah seized and renamed. He grants the numbers are hard to reconcile — Judges 10:3 gives Jair the Gileadite thirty cities, Joshua 13:30 seems to make them sixty — and supposes the tally of the "camps of Jair" simply varied at different epochs.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley attends to the closing clause, "all these belonged to the sons of Machir." The cities fell partly to Machir's own sons and partly to his son-in-law Jair, who — by reason of the dear affection between them, and because he forsook his own tribe to fight for and dwell among them — is here reckoned as one of Machir's own sons. So the witnesses leave two readings of the event, a conquest by Jair or a later loss to the Arameans, but one settled sense of the summary: this cluster of trans-Jordan towns was counted to the Machirite house of Manasseh, whatever hand held it at a given hour.
24:
"And after that Hezron was dead in Calebephratah, then Abiah Hezron's wife bare him Ashur the father of Tekoa." A posthumous son is born to Hezron, and the witnesses find the verse at once tender and textually vexed. The reformed John Gill takes the place-name at face value: Caleb-ephratah is supposed to be the same as Bethlehem, so called from Caleb the son of Hezron and from Ephrath his wife, and there Hezron died, his widow Abiah bearing him a last son, Ashur, who became father — that is, founder or prince — of Tekoa.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch keep the place but confess its obscurity: "in Caleb Ephratah" is a hard phrase; perhaps, as part of the Negeb was called Negeb Caleb (1 Samuel 30:14) because it belonged to Caleb's family, so the town where Caleb dwelt with his wife Ephrath was named Caleb of Ephratah, Ephrath being the ancient name of Bethlehem (Genesis 35:19). Abiah they reckon Hezron's third wife, and Ashur's descendants are traced later, at 1 Chronicles 4:5.

Charles Ellicott judges the text plainly corrupt and follows the Septuagint to a different sense altogether: for "after Hezron was dead in Caleb-ephratah" the Greek read "after Hezron's death Caleb went in to Ephrath," and a change of only three Hebrew letters yields "after Hezron's death Caleb went in to Ephrath, the wife of his father Hezron, and she bare him Ashur." On this reading the son is born not to the dead Hezron but to Caleb by his father's widow — and Ashur, "man of Hur," means the chief of the Hurite clan settled at Bethlehem. Ellicott adds the caution that governs the whole section: "Caleb" here must mean the house of Caleb, since the genealogy makes him great-grandson of Judah, whereas the individual Caleb son of Jephunneh lived four centuries later, at the conquest. So the witnesses hold a place-name reading and a text-critical reading side by side, agreeing only that the verse plants the family of Ashur, father of Tekoa, in the country of Bethlehem — the town of Amos and of the wise woman that Joab fetched to David — and that the corruption, whatever its cure, has not swallowed the plain descent it means to record.
25:
"And the sons of Jerahmeel the firstborn of Hezron were, Ram the firstborn, and Bunah, and Oren, and Ozem, and Ahijah." Having followed Ram and Caleb, the record turns at last to Jerahmeel, Hezron's eldest, and the witnesses meet a fresh name-puzzle in the last word. The reformed John Gill notes only that Jerahmeel's own firstborn was also called Ram, named by his father after his brother (v.9) — a second Ram, easily confused with the first.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place the family: the Jerahmeelites inhabited a part of the Negeb of Judah called after them "the south of the Jerahmeelites" (1 Samuel 27:10). Then they weigh the name Ahijah. Though it occurs elsewhere as a man's name, it is not joined to the others by the copula "and" as they are joined to one another, and so appears to be the name of a woman — probably a letter has dropped out, and it should read "of Ahijah," naming the mother. This gains force, they add, from the next verse, which speaks of "another wife," leading us to expect the first wife named here.

Charles Ellicott presses the same crux and lays out the variants. This Ram, he agrees, is not the Ram brother of Jerahmeel of v.9. As for Ahijah, the conjunction is wanting in the Hebrew, so it is probably no fifth son at all: the Septuagint read "his brother," the Syriac and Arabic "and Ozem their sister," while the likeliest correction, restoring a single dropped letter, gives "from Ahijah" — the former wife who bore these sons. Keil and Delitzsch reinforce the point by counting: five names appear to follow Jerahmeel, but four are linked by the copula and the fifth stands loose, exactly as a mother's name would if a preposition had dropped before it. And they observe that the pattern is regular in these lists — where two wives are mentioned, the first is commonly named at the head of her children — so that the very structure of the passage argues for reading Ahijah as the wife rather than a son. So the witnesses converge: what the English prints as a son's name is more probably the mother of the house, the text bruised by the fall of one small letter, and the true reading recovered only by comparison of the ancient versions and the sense of the whole.
26:
"Jerahmeel had also another wife, whose name was Atarah; she was the mother of Onam." The verse names a second wife and her one recorded son, and the witnesses linger over both the woman's status and her curious name. The reformed John Gill weighs whether Atarah was a true wife, distinct from the one named before, or whether the phrase "another woman" hints, as Kimchi allowed, at a harlot or concubine; but Gill judges the former sense the better — a lawful second wife.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch order the household: only one son of this second wife is given, Onam, whose posterity follows at vv.28-33, while the intervening v.27 first finishes the three sons of Ram, the firstborn by the earlier wife. So the two branches of Jerahmeel's house are set out in turn, the elder wife's and the younger's.

Charles Ellicott turns to the name itself. Atarah means a crown or "corona," the same word used again at v.54; probably, he suggests, it denotes the ring-fence or fortifications that crowned a city, much as the Greeks used their word for a crown of the walls of Troy. The plural, Ataroth, indeed appears as an actual town-name (Numbers 32:3). The hint is characteristic of Ellicott's whole approach to the chapter: that many of these "names" are as much places as persons, the genealogy shading continually into a map of Judah's towns. Keil and Delitzsch keep the household in order against this, noting that though only Onam is named as Atarah's son, the writer reserves him for full treatment and first completes the elder wife's line at v.27, so that neither branch is confused with the other. Whether Atarah was simply a woman so named, or her name preserves the memory of a fortified place from which her family came, the witnesses cannot finally say; but with her son Onam the Jerahmeelite line gains the branch that will run on, through Shammai and Jada, to the house of Sheshan and its daughters. The reformed and the grammatical-historical voices thus meet on the substance — a second wife, a single son, and the longer descent that son begins — while differing only on the shade of meaning the name Atarah may carry.
27:
"And the sons of Ram the firstborn of Jerahmeel were, Maaz, and Jamin, and Eker." A single verse disposes of the firstborn's branch, and the witnesses note how quickly it closes. The reformed John Gill adds only that these three — Maaz, Jamin, and Eker — were the sons of Ram by Jerahmeel's first wife, the house of the elder line.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the branch in its place within the larger design. Ram is the firstborn of Jerahmeel, and his three sons are named here; yet the record does not follow them a step further. It is the line of the second son, Onam, that the genealogy pursues at length (vv.28-33), down through Shammai and Nadab and Appaim to Sheshan and the daughters that will presently occupy the writer's whole attention. Maaz, Jamin, and Eker are named and left; of their descendants nothing is said.

The witnesses find in this a pattern that has run through the chapter from the first. Again and again the elder or the more obvious branch is set down in a word and then dropped, while the line that carries the interest springs from a younger son or a later wife — Pharez over Zerah, Ram's family traced before Jerahmeel's, and now, within Jerahmeel's own house, the firstborn Ram's three sons passed over for the sake of Onam's longer posterity. The Chronicler is no mere antiquarian filling every branch of the tree; he prunes hard, keeping what serves his purpose and letting the rest fall away. Gill's small note, that these were Ram's sons by the first wife, quietly marks the same forking that Keil and Delitzsch trace in the design: the elder wife's line here reaches its end, while the second wife's line, through Onam, is the one the writer will pursue at length. The three bare names of this verse are a reminder that in the genealogies, as in the histories, God's choice does not always rest on the firstborn, and that many a house is remembered by Scripture for a single line in a register and no more — named once, that it may not be wholly forgotten, and then left in the silence out of which the chosen line goes on.
28:
"And the sons of Onam were, Shammai, and Jada. And the sons of Shammai; Nadab, and Abishur." The record now takes up the branch it means to follow, and the witnesses read the verse as the doorway into the fuller Jerahmeelite pedigree. The reformed John Gill marks the descent simply: these are the sons of Onam, the son whom Jerahmeel had by his other wife, Atarah (v.26).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the branching that follows. Onam had two sons, Shammai and Jada; and of Shammai's two sons, Nadab and Abishur, the line runs on — Nadab's posterity ending, after four generations, in one Ahlai, whom they judge to be not a son but a daughter, the heiress of Sheshan (v.34), since Sheshan had no sons but only daughters. Thus the two sons of Shammai open the two strands the chapter will pursue: Nadab toward Sheshan and the Egyptian marriage, Abishur toward the sons his wife Abihail would bear him.

The witnesses see in this careful forking the Chronicler's method in miniature. Onam's is the line chosen over his elder brother Ram's; and within it the writer will follow every turn — the wives named, the childless noted, the daughters counted — because it leads to the striking episode of Sheshan, who, lacking sons, gave his daughter to an Egyptian bondman that his house might not die out. Nothing here touches the royal line of David; these are the obscure clans of the southern desert, the Jerahmeelites of the Negeb. Yet the Chronicler records them with the same patience he gave to the ancestors of the king, and the witnesses take the lesson that in the book of God's remembrance the small houses of Israel are numbered as faithfully as the great, each name kept though the world has long forgotten it. Keil and Delitzsch's care to sort Shammai's two sons — the one whose posterity ends in a daughter, the other whose wife bears him children — shows that even here, among names met nowhere else in Scripture, the writer means to be exact; for it is this very line, and no other, that will yield the arresting story of a house saved from extinction by the marriage of a daughter to a foreign slave.
29:
"And the name of the wife of Abishur was Abihail, and she bare him Ahban, and Molid." The genealogy pauses to name a wife, and the witnesses note how the women of these obscure clans are set down with the same care as the men. The reformed John Gill remarks that Abihail was a name borne elsewhere too — a wife of Rehoboam was so called, a daughter of Jesse's eldest son Eliab, David's brother (2 Chronicles 11:18) — though this Abihail, of the Jerahmeelite house, is another woman.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix her place in the line: to Abishur, the younger son of Shammai (v.28), his wife Abihail bore two sons, Ahban and Molid, and with them his branch of the family terminates — a short offshoot that flowers and then ends. The record turns back, in the verses that follow, to the elder son Nadab, whose line runs on toward Sheshan.

The witnesses find something quietly telling in the naming of Abihail. In a register that could easily have listed only fathers and sons, the Chronicler stops to record the wife by name — as he does again and again in this chapter, with Azubah and Ephrath and Atarah, with Ephah and Maachah the concubines, and soon with the daughter of Sheshan. It is a mark, the witnesses observe, of how the Hebrew genealogies honour the mothers who carried the houses forward; the women are not passed over as mere instruments but named as members of the covenant people. Abihail bears two sons and her husband's branch reaches its end; yet her name is written, and by that writing she is remembered among the families of Judah when Ahban and Molid, of whom nothing else is told, would otherwise have no memorial at all. Gill's cross-reference to the other Abihail, the granddaughter of Jesse who married into the royal house (2 Chronicles 11:18), further shows how the genealogists kept even the recurrence of a woman's name in view, tracing it from clan to clan; and Keil and Delitzsch's note that Abishur's race here terminates gives the verse its quiet shape — a marriage, two sons, and then the line's peaceful close, before the record returns to the branch of Nadab that runs on.
30:
"And the sons of Nadab; Seled, and Appaim: but Seled died without children." The elder son's line is resumed, and at once one of its two branches fails. The reformed John Gill marks the descent plainly: these are the sons of Nadab, the elder son of Shammai (v.28) — Seled and Appaim — of whom Seled died without children, leaving no posterity to trace.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the surviving line forward: from Appaim came Ishi, from Ishi Sheshan, and from Sheshan Ahlai (v.31) — the narrow thread by which the house is kept alive, since Seled's branch is cut off at once. So the whole weight of Nadab's posterity rests on the younger of his two sons.

Charles Ellicott adds a reading that lifts the note above mere family record. "Seled died without children," he suggests, means that the clan of Seled did not multiply and subdivide into new groups — the language of a tribe that failed to grow rather than only of a man who left no heir, and he compares the like phrase at v.32, where Jether too "died without children." On this understanding the genealogy is charting the rise and extinction of whole families in the Negeb: some houses branch and flourish, others dwindle and vanish. The witnesses do not press the two senses against each other, for a childless man and a clan that does not multiply come in the end to the same thing. But Ellicott's angle reminds the reader that these registers are the demographic memory of a people — a record of which houses of Judah endured and which quietly died away — and that the Chronicler notes the failures as faithfully as the fruitful lines. Keil and Delitzsch's tracing of the surviving thread down to Ahlai, whom they take to be Sheshan's daughter and heiress rather than a son, sharpens the same lesson: the line of Appaim narrows almost to nothing, a single descendant in each generation, until it reaches a house that will have no sons at all. That a family so near extinction should yet be preserved — and preserved, as the coming verses tell, through a daughter and a stranger — is, for the witnesses, the point toward which this bare notice of Seled's childlessness is already tending.
31:
"And the sons of Appaim; Ishi. And the sons of Ishi; Sheshan. And the children of Sheshan; Ahlai." Three times the plural "sons" or "children" governs a single name, and the witnesses fasten on that oddity of Hebrew idiom. The reformed John Gill notes it first: though each of these men had but one son, the plural number is used, the posterity being included under the head of the house — a usage he finds again in the next clause.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that this is no error but a manner of speech, one often met even in profane authors, where "sons" is written though there be but one son. The plural, in other words, names not a count of children but the line that descends from a man, whether it pass through one heir or many.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses further, and here the reformed and grammatical readings part a little. Ahlai, he holds, is the name of a clan, not of an individual; and he sets aside the older expedients — that "sons of so-and-so" is a mere conventional phrase used even for one person, or that the Chronicler has abbreviated his source by keeping only a single name — as antiquated. Yet Ahlai is precisely the name that v.34 forces the reader to reconsider, for there Sheshan is said to have had no sons but daughters. So a real question opens over this last name: is Ahlai a son, or a daughter and heiress, or the designation of a clan? Ellicott grounds his clan-reading by comparison with the like phrases he had discussed earlier in the register (1 Chronicles 1:41), where a single name follows "the sons of" and is best taken as a family rather than a person. The witnesses do not settle the matter here; but they agree that the plural "children of Sheshan" cannot be pressed to mean a houseful of boys, and that the single name it introduces is the hinge on which the strange history of the next verses will turn — a house whose whole future hangs, as it proves, upon an only child, and whose survival will be owed not to a son of the blood but to a daughter and a stranger.
32:
"And the sons of Jada the brother of Shammai; Jether, and Jonathan: and Jether died without children." The record turns back to complete the other branch of Onam's house, and once more a line fails at once. The reformed John Gill marks the descent plainly: these are the sons of Jada, the brother of Shammai (v.28) — Jether and Jonathan — of whom Jether, like Seled before him (v.30), died without children, so that his brother Jonathan alone carries the line on.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that with Jonathan's sons the whole Jerahmeelite genealogy is rounded off, the register closing at v.33 with the subscription, "these were the sons of Jerahmeel." They report, too, the count that Bertheau drew from the section: reckoning the descendants of Judah through Zerah, through Ram down to David, through Caleb, and through Jerahmeel, the whole comes to seventy — that round and symbolic number, though it is reached only by weighing which persons are to be counted and which passed over.

The witnesses find the repeated note of childlessness quietly instructive. Twice within a few verses a son of this house "died without children" — Seled and now Jether — and each time the burden of the line falls upon a single surviving brother. The genealogy does not hide these failures; it records the branches that came to nothing as carefully as those that flourished, so that the survival of the Jerahmeelite house appears as a thread drawn again and again to the very edge of breaking. Keil and Delitzsch are careful to add that the number seventy is not reached mechanically; Bertheau attains it only by counting the father and mother of Amasa as two persons, against the usual rule that the father alone is reckoned unless the mother be the more famous — so that even the round total rests on a judgment about whom to include. That the count should nonetheless fall out to seventy, the number of Jacob's household that went down into Egypt, is for the witnesses a fitting seal: the tribe of Judah, for all its blighted and broken branches, is preserved whole in the reckoning of God, not one of its true houses lost from the count, and its register bearing the same sum that once measured the whole family of Israel.
33:
"And the sons of Jonathan; Peleth, and Zaza. These were the sons of Jerahmeel." The Jerahmeelite register reaches its formal close, and the witnesses treat the verse as both an ending and a threshold. The reformed John Gill remarks of the two last names, Peleth and Zaza, only that no mention is made of them anywhere else — obscure heads of an obscure clan, remembered by this single notice and nothing more.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the subscription, "these were the sons of Jerahmeel," as drawing a line under the whole genealogy of Hezron's firstborn, which has run from v.25 through its several branches. But the same witnesses see that the register is not quite done, for it now doubles back to a name already met — Sheshan — to tell the one story in this desert genealogy that rises above a list. Sheshan, they observe, had no sons but daughters, and possessed an Egyptian servant named Jarha (v.34); to him Sheshan gave his daughter, and from that marriage sprang a long posterity traced through Attai and Nathan and Zabad and beyond.

So the closing formula of this verse is less a full stop than a hinge. The witnesses note the artistry of it: having numbered the ordinary branches of Jerahmeel to their end, the Chronicler returns to the one house whose survival hung by a thread, and unfolds it at length. Peleth and Zaza vanish into silence with the subscription; but Sheshan's line, saved from extinction by an unlooked-for marriage, will run on for generation after generation. Keil and Delitzsch follow that preserved line down its length, through Attai and Nathan and Zabad and the generations after, to show that from so precarious a beginning a durable posterity in fact sprang. The reader is thus brought to feel, at the very seam of the register, the difference between the houses that simply ended and the one that God preserved by a strange providence — a difference the next verses will make the more striking by the humbleness of the means, an Egyptian bondman set free and married to his master's only child, that a family of Judah might not perish from the earth.
34:
"Now Sheshan had no sons, but daughters. And Sheshan had a servant, an Egyptian, whose name was Jarha." The verse sets the stage for the section's one memorable story, and the witnesses attend both to the textual snag it creates and to the remarkable marriage it prepares. The reformed John Gill faces the snag first. Sheshan had daughters only — and, it seems, but one, for at v.31 his child was named Ahlai, the plural there standing for the singular. Or, Gill allows, if Ahlai was indeed a son, he must have died in his father's lifetime and left no issue, so that only the daughter remained.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the difficulty out squarely: v.31 called Ahlai one of the "children of Sheshan," yet here Sheshan is said to have no sons. Those who insist on reading the lists literally reconcile the two by making Ahlai a daughter; others suppose the Chronicler has preserved fragments of at least two independent accounts, not perfectly harmonised. Ellicott leaves both possibilities open, characteristically preferring to expose the seam rather than to smooth it over.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns to the servant Jarha, and relays the Targum's addition that fills the story out: his master "gave him his liberty, and gave him Sheshan his daughter to wife," so that the marriage was preceded by the man's release from bondage. The witnesses thus hold together a small critical problem and a large human one. Sheshan's house is at the point of failure, with no son to carry the name; the only heir is a daughter; and the man at hand is an Egyptian bondservant. Whether Ahlai be that daughter or a lost son, and whether the two notices spring from one source or two, the plain drift of the verse is clear enough — a Judahite family, faced with extinction, is about to be preserved by an expedient at once bold and lowly, the manumission and marriage of a foreign slave. The witnesses feel the pathos of it: a house with no son and but one daughter, and at hand no kinsman but an Egyptian in bondage, upon whom, freed and wedded, the whole future of the line will come to rest.
35:
"And Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha his servant to wife; and she bare him Attai." The bold expedient is carried out, and the witnesses dwell on both the identity of the daughter and the worth of the man. The reformed John Gill notes that Sheshan first gave Jarha his freedom, as the Targum premised, and then his daughter; and this daughter, he judges, was Ahlai herself — a conclusion confirmed by the fact that Zabad, a descendant of this marriage, is later called "the son of Ahlai" (1 Chronicles 11:41), that is, her great-grandson, so that the whole line is reckoned from the mother.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley weighs what manner of man this Jarha must have been. He was probably not only a proselyte but an eminent one; else an Israelite would never have given him his only daughter, and with her the continuance of his house and the inheritance of his name. The marriage, on Wesley's reading, was no desperate mismatch but the joining of the heiress to a worthy convert.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, in his survey of the chapter, had already drawn out the lesson of it: here is one who had an only daughter and married her to his servant, an Egyptian — and if it is recorded to his praise, we must suppose the Egyptian was a proselyte to the true religion and eminent for wisdom and virtue, for otherwise a true-born Israelite would not have matched his daughter to him. If Egyptians become converts and servants do worthily, Henry says, neither their parentage nor their servitude should bar their preferment; such a one this servant might be, that she who married him lived as happily as if she had married one of the rulers of her tribe. So the witnesses make of this quiet verse a small gospel of grace: a foreigner and a bondman, received into the covenant by faith, is honoured to carry forward a house of Judah, his name blotted from the record while the line runs on through his wife — a stranger grafted in, that Sheshan's house might live.
42:
"Now the sons of Caleb the brother of Jerahmeel were Mesha his firstborn, which was the father of Ziph; and the sons of Mareshah the father of Hebron." Having finished the Jerahmeelites, the record returns to Caleb for a fresh list of his descendants, and the witnesses begin by settling, once more, which Caleb is meant. The reformed John Gill identifies him as Chelubai, the same Caleb already treated at v.18, and reckons these later sons to be by another wife.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is emphatic on the point of confusion: this was not Caleb the son of Jephunneh, the spy and warrior, but Caleb the son of Hezron (v.18; v.50). Some, he notes, think Caleb the son of Hezron was the grandson of Caleb the son of Jephunneh, but this he judges probably fanciful. On the phrase "the father of Ziph" Clarke passes on the Targum's gloss, "the prince of the Ziphites" — the first hint of what the whole coming list will confirm, that these "fathers" are the heads or founders of towns.

The witnesses see at once the character this new register will bear. Mesha is father of Ziph, and Mareshah father of Hebron — names that are as much places as persons, the towns of the Judean hill-country appearing under the guise of men. Ziph and Hebron and Mareshah were real cities, and to call a man their "father" is to name him their founder or chief. So the genealogy here shades openly into geography, tracing not merely a family tree but the settlement of Judah's south by the clans of Caleb. The Chronicler, the witnesses observe, is mapping the tribe onto its land: each name a household, and each household planted in its town, so that the register of Caleb's sons is at the same time a gazetteer of the country David would one day rule from Hebron. Gill's identifying of these later sons as by another wife, and Clarke's firm severing of this Caleb from the famous spy, together clear the ground for reading the list aright — not as a rival account of Caleb son of Jephunneh, but as the spreading of the Hezronite Caleb's house through Ziph and Mareshah and Hebron, the southern towns that his clans would hold for generations.
43:
"And the sons of Hebron; Korah, and Tappuah, and Rekem, and Shema." The town named a father in the verse before now appears itself as a father of sons, and the witnesses read the paradox as the key to the whole passage. The reformed John Gill points out that these names too are places: Tappuah is the name of a city in Judah, and there is also a Beth-tappuah in the same tribe, one or both of which may have taken their name from this man; and Shema likewise answers to a town of the south.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the line on through the verses that follow — Shema begetting Raham the father of Jorkoam, Rekem begetting Shammai, and so down to the concubines of Caleb and their sons — treating the whole as the spreading of Caleb's house into a cluster of Judean settlements. Hebron here stands as a "son" of Caleb, though Hebron was among the most ancient and famous cities of the land; the genealogy does not scruple to set a great city in the place of a man, because its purpose is to show which Calebite clan held which town.

The witnesses find in this the settled method of the section. Persons and places have become almost interchangeable: a clan is named for its founder, and the founder for the town his clan possessed, so that "the sons of Hebron" means the families that peopled Hebron and its dependent villages. The effect, they note, is to bind the tribe of Judah to its soil. Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema are not merely names in a list but the memory of how the hill-country was parcelled out among the houses of Caleb — a record precious to the restored community, who could trace their own towns and holdings back through these very clans to the great ancestor whose name the register bears. Gill's noticing that Tappuah answers both to a Tappuah and to a Beth-tappuah in Judah illustrates the whole method at a stroke: a single clan-name could cling to more than one settlement, the family leaving its mark wherever its households spread. So the witnesses read "the sons of Hebron" less as a nursery of children than as a chart of the country, the great southern city and its lesser neighbours all gathered, under the figure of sonship, into the one Calebite stock.
44:
"And Shema begat Raham, the father of Jorkoam: and Rekem begat Shammai." The register runs on through another pair of names, and the witnesses read them by the rule that governs the whole Calebite list. The reformed John Gill notes that Jorkoam, whose "father" Raham is called, is taken by Hillerus to be the name of a city in the tribe of Judah; and he passes on Jarchi's governing observation, that wherever the word "father" is used in these verses it is to be understood of the prince or chief of the city that follows the name.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the two links within the branching descent of Caleb's house, tracing Shema and Rekem, the sons of Hebron (v.43), each to a son of his own — Raham and Shammai — and on toward the further generations and the concubines' children that the section will reach. The line, in their hands, is a steady widening of the clan across the country.

Jarchi's principle, which Gill records, unlocks the verse and much of what surrounds it. To say "Raham, the father of Jorkoam" is not to name a man and his infant son but to name the head under whom a town and its people stood — Jorkoam a place, Raham its founder or ruler. The witnesses see in this a genealogy written from the standpoint of the land: the families of Caleb are remembered chiefly by the settlements they held, and the "begettings" trace as much the founding of villages as the birth of children. The witnesses note how Gill and Keil and Delitzsch here reinforce one another: the reformed commentator supplies the interpretive key from Jarchi and Hillerus, that a name followed by "the father of" a place marks the ruler of a town, and the grammatical-historical pair supply the frame, tracing how the sons of Hebron branched into these further heads. So even these two obscure names, met nowhere else in Scripture, carry their small freight of history — a clan of Judah, a town it built or governed, and the long memory of the Chronicler that would not let either be wholly lost, but bound them into the register of the tribe from which the King would come.
45:
"And the son of Shammai was Maon: and Maon was the father of Bethzur." Two more links, and two more towns of Judah, are folded into the Calebite line, and the witnesses read them by the now-familiar principle. The reformed John Gill observes that Maon gave its name to a city in the tribe of Judah — the Maon of the southern wilderness — and that, as before, to call Maon "the father of Bethzur" is to name him the founder or prince of that town.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place the descent in order: Shammai, whom the previous verse reached, begat Maon, and Maon in turn stands as father of Beth-zur, so that clan and town descend together, the one bearing the other's name. Beth-zur was a real stronghold of the Judean hills, and its appearance here as the "son" of Maon is of a piece with the whole section, in which the settlements of the south are ranged under the Calebite houses that held them.

The witnesses draw once more the lesson that these verses press by their very form. The genealogy has become a map: Ziph, Hebron, Tappuah, Jorkoam, Maon, Beth-zur — town after town appears as a person, each fathered by the clan that founded or governed it, until the reader sees the hill-country of Judah spread out as a family tree. The Chronicler, writing to a people newly returned to that land, gives them in these names the title-deeds of their inheritance, showing how the towns they dwelt in had come down to them through the sons of Caleb. Gill's simple identifying of Maon as a Judean town, and Keil and Delitzsch's ordering of the descent from Shammai through Maon to Beth-zur, together turn the verse from a bare pair of names into a fragment of the tribe's settlement-history. What looks at first like a dry chain of "begettings" is in truth the memory of a tribe's possession of its soil — the same soil, and the same hill-town of Hebron, from which David would first be anointed king over Judah. The witnesses take it as no accident that a genealogy so bent toward David should also be a survey of David's own country, the towns of the south named one by one as if to say that the land and the line were given together, and kept together, by the God who had promised both to the house of Judah.
46:
"And Ephah, Caleb's concubine, bare Haran, and Moza, and Gazez: and Haran begat Gazez." The record turns from Caleb's sons by his wives to his sons by his concubines, and the witnesses handle both the domestic arrangement and a small genealogical riddle. The reformed John Gill explains the relation: Ephah was a concubine, a half-wife or secondary wife; for though this Caleb seems not to have had more than one wife at a time, he kept concubines beside them, and the record will name another, if not a third, before the section is done.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the descendants of Caleb by these two concubines, and observe the strangeness of the closing clause. Ephah bore three sons — Haran, of whom nothing further is known; Moza, a name borne elsewhere by a Benjamite town (Joshua 18:26) but not therefore a town here; and Gazez, otherwise unknown. Then the verse ends, oddly, "and Haran begat Gazez," repeating the name. This second Gazez, they suggest, was perhaps a grandson of Caleb; either the clause "Haran begat Gazez" is a mere explanatory addition, or Haran named his own son after his younger brother, so that a son and a grandson of Caleb bore the same name.

The witnesses find the very awkwardness a mark of honesty. The Chronicler does not tidy his sources into a smooth line but sets down what he found, repetition and all, even where a name recurs within a single verse and puzzles the reader. Ephah's status as concubine is stated without apology, her three sons numbered among Caleb's house as truly as the children of his wives; and the doubled Gazez, whether an editorial gloss or a grandson named for an uncle, is left standing. Keil and Delitzsch's patience with the knot — refusing to cut it, content to lay out the possibilities — is itself instructive, showing how the genealogist worked, gathering the memory of a great household with all its concubines and half-remembered grandsons, and preserving it whole rather than trimming it to a neat descent. The reformed and grammatical-historical voices thus meet on the substance: an honest record of Caleb's fuller family, in which even the secondary wives and their obscure sons are counted, and even a small textual puzzle is left standing rather than smoothed away.
47:
"And the sons of Jahdai; Regem, and Jotham, and Geshan, and Pelet, and Ephah, and Shaaph." A name appears here with no thread tying it to what precedes, and the witnesses confess the break. The reformed John Gill observes that Jahdai has not been mentioned before under this name; perhaps he is the same as Moza (v.46), who might have borne two names, or a son of Moza, as Hillerus thought; or perhaps Jahdai is the name of yet another of Caleb's concubines, by whom he had these six sons.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch state the difficulty without softening it: the genealogical connection of these names is entirely wanting, for Jahdai appears quite abruptly, with no word to say whose son or husband he is. Hiller's guess that Jahdai is another name for Moza they judge to be without sufficient ground. They note too the recurrence of names within the section — Ephah, here a son of Jahdai, is elsewhere the name of a Midianite chief (1 Chronicles 1:33), and Shaaph appears again, of another man, in the very next stretch of the list (v.49).

The witnesses draw from this abruptness an honest estimate of the genealogy's state. It is not a seamless tree but a gathering of fragments, some of them broken at the join, where a source has been quoted whose beginning is lost, so that Jahdai stands orphaned in the text with his six sons and no visible parent. Rather than invent a link, the commentators let the gap show; and rather than force the recurring names into a single identity, they mark that Ephah and Shaaph are common names, borne by different men in different clans. The Chronicler, they imply, preserved what came to him even when it came without its connections — a reminder that these ancient registers reach us worn and partial, faithful records of a real people whose full family lines time had already begun to blur even before the book was written. Gill's readiness to guess — that Jahdai might be Moza, or his son, or a further concubine — and Keil and Delitzsch's refusal to guess where the evidence fails, together model the honesty the passage demands: better a frank blank than a forced link, and better a name left orphaned than a false father supplied to it.
48:
"Maachah, Caleb's concubine, bare Sheber, and Tirhanah." Another concubine is named, and the witnesses note both the frequency of her name and a small grammatical surprise. The reformed John Gill remarks only that Maachah was another concubine of Caleb, a third such woman in the household after Ephah and Jahdai.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that Maachah is a not uncommon woman's name, borne by several persons elsewhere in the book (compare 1 Chronicles 3:2 and 1 Chronicles 7:16); her two sons, Sheber and Tirhanah, are names quite unknown, met nowhere else. They point out a nicety of the Hebrew: the verb "bare" here stands in the masculine form, where the feminine was used of Ephah at v.46 — a shift they explain by supposing that the father who begat, rather than the mother who bore, was present to the writer's mind as he set the clause down.

The witnesses see in such details the working of a real and careful hand. The concubines of Caleb are each named in turn, their sons numbered, though of Sheber and Tirhanah the record has nothing more to tell; and even the gender of a verb is worth the grammarian's notice, betraying how the writer's thought slid from mother to father as he wrote. Keil and Delitzsch carry the line straight on, for the same Maachah bears also, in the next verse, Shaaph the father of Madmannah and Sheva the father of Machbenah, so that this concubine becomes the ancestress of several Judean towns. The witnesses thus refuse to treat these verses as lifeless. Behind the bare names stands a great household of the south, its wives and concubines and many sons remembered clan by clan; and the Chronicler, gathering them, honours even the secondary wives and their forgotten children with a place in the register of the people of God. Gill's plain reckoning of Maachah as a third concubine, set beside Keil and Delitzsch's grammarian's eye for the masculine verb, shows the two schools working the same soil from different sides — the one numbering the household's members, the other listening to the very cadence of the Hebrew — and both content that Sheber and Tirhanah, of whom Scripture says no more, should yet be kept in the roll. That a concubine's unknown sons become, through the next verse, the fathers of towns is for the witnesses a token of how the humblest names in the list may still root themselves in the land of Judah.
49:
