"She bare also Shaaph the father of Madmannah, Sheva the father of Machbenah, and the father of Gibea: and the daughter of Caleb was Achsah." The concubine Maachah's line runs on into more towns of the south, and the section adds a daughter at its close. The reformed John Gill reads the names by the now-settled rule: Shaaph, father of Madmannah, was prince of a place so called in the tribe of Judah, and the like holds of Machbenah and Gibea.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states the principle outright: Madmannah, he says, and divers other of the following names, are the names of places in Judah — so that the reader is not to look for a nursery of children but for a map of the country, its towns ranged under the clans that held them.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add the geography. Madmannah is a city in the south of Judah, perhaps to be identified with a site south of Gaza (Joshua 15:31); Machbenah is a village of Judah not further named; and Gibea may be the Gibeah of the Judean hill-country (Joshua 15:57). Then the list closes with a person, not a place: "the daughter of Caleb was Achsah." The witnesses note the quiet turn. After a long muster of sons who are really towns, the register ends on a single named daughter — a reminder that behind the geography stand real households with real children. The Chronicler sets Achsah at the foot of the Calebite list much as the histories set the daughter of the elder Caleb at the winning of her field; and whether or not the two are to be linked, the effect is the same, that a woman's name crowns the record of Caleb's house, and the whole spreading clan of the south is gathered up in the end to father and daughter. The witnesses observe how the three voices converge on the verse's double character: Gill and Wesley pressing that Madmannah, Machbenah, and Gibea are towns and their "fathers" the princes who held them, Keil and Delitzsch fixing those towns on the map of southern Judah, and all three noting that the section, having ranged town after town under the guise of sonship, turns at the last to name a real daughter, so that the map does not quite swallow the household, and Achsah stands at the foot of the list as a person among the places.
50:
"These were the sons of Caleb the son of Hur, the firstborn of Ephratah; Shobal the father of Kirjathjearim." A new heading opens the last movement of the chapter, and the witnesses divide at once over how to construe it. The reformed John Gill reads it as introducing yet a third Caleb: another Caleb, he says, the grandson of Caleb the son of Hezron and called after his name, the son of Hur, who was the firstborn of Caleb's wife Ephratah (v.19). On this view the register now turns from the elder Caleb to a younger namesake in the family.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the same words quite differently. The heading "these are the sons of Caleb," they urge, is defined by what follows, "the son of Hur, the firstborn of Ephratah"; but this is not an apposition making a new "Caleb the son of Hur." Rather, since v.19 has already shown that Hur was a son of Caleb by Ephrath, the phrase limits the coming lists to the families descended from Caleb through his son Hur. There is no third Caleb; there is the one Caleb, whose descendants by Hur, the firstborn of Ephratah, are now to be told — the same Hur whose grandson was Bezaleel (v.20).

So a genuine question stands between the witnesses: does the verse name a grandson Caleb, or does it gather the descendants of the elder Caleb through Hur? The stakes are small for the sense of the whole, since either way the following names — Shobal father of Kirjath-jearim, and the rest — belong to the Calebite stock rooted at Ephratah, that is, Bethlehem. But the difference shows the care each school brings. Gill multiplies the Calebs to keep the plain grammar of "Caleb the son of Hur"; Keil and Delitzsch reduce them, reading the clause in the light of v.19, and finding here not a new man but a fresh branch of an old house. The witnesses leave the reader to choose, agreeing only that the last families of the chapter spring from the line of Hur at Bethlehem.
51:
"Salma the father of Bethlehem, Hareph the father of Bethgader." Among the sons of Hur one name shines with a light the rest have not, for Salma is called the father of Bethlehem, the town of David and, in time, of David's greater Son. The reformed John Gill marks the honour and guards against a confusion: this Salma is father, or as the Targum has it prince, of Bethlehem — but he is not the Salma of the royal line, the son of Nahshon (v.11) who begat Boaz; this is a later Salma, of the younger Calebite house.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads "father of Bethlehem" in the collective sense the section has taught: it means the inhabitants of Bethlehem — Salma standing as head of the clan that peopled the town. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place both names in the descent of Hur's line: Salma father of Bethlehem, Hareph father of Beth-gader, two more of the Calebite chiefs whose households founded or held the villages of the Judean hills.

The witnesses feel the quiet weight of Bethlehem's appearing here. The Calebite line, which is not the royal line, nonetheless plants itself in the very town from which the King would come, so that two streams of Judah — the house of Nahshon and Boaz through whom David descended, and the house of Hur and Salma who fathered Bethlehem — meet in one small city. Gill's care to distinguish the two Salmas keeps the genealogy exact; but the witnesses do not miss the providence that set the founding of Bethlehem within Caleb's register, hard by the roll of David's own ancestors. Ephrath, which is Bethlehem (Micah 5:2), has been the hidden refrain of this whole Calebite section, and now it is named outright, the town of the coming King owned as the possession of the sons of Hur. Wesley's collective reading — that "father of Bethlehem" means the men who peopled it — and Keil and Delitzsch's placing of Salma and Hareph in Hur's descent, join with Gill's careful distinction of the two Salmas to give the verse its full sense: not that one man begat a city, but that a Calebite clan founded and filled the very place where, generations later, a virgin of David's house would lay her firstborn in a manger.
52:
"And Shobal the father of Kirjathjearim had sons; Haroeh, and half of the Manahethites." The register turns to Shobal and his descendants, and the witnesses read the verse first as a lesson in how these "fathers" are to be understood. The reformed John Gill observes that the very phrasing — "Shobal the father of Kirjathjearim had sons" — proves that Kirjath-jearim is not the name of a man, nor of one of Shobal's sons, but of a place of which Shobal was prince; for a town cannot itself have sons, and so the "father of Kirjathjearim" must be its chief, whose actual children the verse goes on to name.

The clause that follows, however, is obscure, and the witnesses handle it with care. Keil and Delitzsch (in their comment on these verses) take "Haroeh and half of the Manahethites" to be proper names, the sons or clans that sprang from Shobal, and connect Haroeh with the Reaiah who is called a son of Shobal at 1 Chronicles 4:2. The Manahethites are a family named again, in their other half, at the chapter's close (v.54), so that a single clan is divided between two Calebite houses.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke records the Targum's fanciful expansion, that Shobal's "sons" were "disciples and priests, to whom belonged the half of the oblations" — a reading that turns the household into a company of religious functionaries. The witnesses do not follow the Targum's flight, but they let it stand as a curiosity, and return to the plainer sense. Shobal, prince of Kirjath-jearim — the town where the ark would one day rest — is the head of a clan whose branches, the seeing Reaiah and half the Manahethites, are here set down. The genealogy keeps its steady work, binding a famous Judean town and its ruling family into the great Calebite stock, and the reader is once more shown a person and a place fused into one name. Gill's insistence on the grammar and Keil and Delitzsch's recovery of the proper names behind an obscure clause work to the same end, that the verse be read as sober record and not, with the Targum, as pious romance; and the ark's future resting-place at Kirjath-jearim gives the small notice its quiet dignity, a town that would one day shelter the presence of God here entered simply among the holdings of Shobal's house.
53:
"And the families of Kirjathjearim; the Ithrites, and the Puhites, and the Shumathites, and the Mishraites; of them came the Zareathites, and the Eshtaulites." The verse unfolds Shobal's house into a cluster of clans, and the witnesses trace both their descent and their later fame. The reformed John Gill notes that these are the families that dwelt at Kirjath-jearim, of which Shobal was prince, and which sprang from him.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify what they can. The Ithrites are the family to which David's mighty men Ira and Gareb belonged (2 Samuel 23:38; 1 Chronicles 11:40), so that this obscure Calebite clan yielded two of the king's heroes; the other three families are met nowhere else. From these four, they add, came the Zareathites and the Eshtaulites, the inhabitants of Zorah and Eshtaol (Joshua 15:33) — Zorah being the home of Samson (Judges 13:2), the town whose ruin, in Keil's day, still bore a like name.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke passes on the Targum's bold romance: these families, it says, were "the children of Moses, which Zipporah bare to him," and of them came the disciples of the prophets at Zorah and Eshtaol — a tradition that would make the Calebite clans of Kirjath-jearim the offspring of the great lawgiver himself. The witnesses do not credit the fancy, but they mark how these small names reach out into the wider Scripture. From the families of one Judean town spring warriors of David and neighbours of Samson, so that the dry list opens, at a touch, upon the histories of the judges and the kingdom. The Chronicler's patient register, the witnesses see, is no backwater; its clans are the very stock from which Israel's deliverers and champions were drawn, and to trace them is to walk among the roots of the nation's story. Gill's plain word that these families sprang from Shobal, and Keil and Delitzsch's identifications drawn from the histories, together turn a string of otherwise blank names — Puhites, Shumathites, Mishraites — into living links: the Ithrites who gave David two of his mighty men, the Zorites and Eshtaulites who were Samson's own neighbours. The witnesses thus refuse to read the verse as lifeless antiquarianism; behind each clan-name lies a town, a history, and a share in the long work by which God raised up judges and warriors to save his people, so that even here the genealogy quietly serves the story of redemption.
54:
"The sons of Salma; Bethlehem, and the Netophathites, Ataroth, the house of Joab, and half of the Manahethites, the Zorites." The register returns to Salma, father of Bethlehem (v.51), and lists his "sons," which the witnesses again read as clans and towns rather than persons. The reformed John Gill takes Bethlehem here for its inhabitants, at least many of them, of whom Salma was prince, and the Netophathites for the people of Netophah, a place of Judah named alongside Bethlehem, who sprang from Salma.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch sort the cluster. The Netophathites dwelt near Bethlehem — a family that produced renowned men at various times (2 Samuel 23:28; 2 Kings 25:23; Ezra 2:22; Nehemiah 7:26). "Ataroth, the house of Joab" they take as the name of a place standing for its inhabitants; the half of the Manahethites answers to the other half met at v.52, near Manahath (1 Chronicles 8:6); and the Zorites are but another form of the Zoreathites, the men of Zorah.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke relays the Targum's elaborate legend of the Netophathites: that they were righteous men who removed the guards Jeroboam had set to stop the people carrying first-fruits to Jerusalem, cleaving wood into ladders to bring their offerings up secretly, and that they came of the line of Joab the son of Zeruiah, some of them priests. The witnesses treat the tale as haggadah, not history, yet note how it fastens on the honour of the name. What the plain text gives is a knot of Bethlehemite and neighbouring clans — Netophah, Ataroth of the house of Joab, half the Manahethites, the Zorites — all reckoned to Salma, and so the whole countryside about Bethlehem gathered into the Calebite house that fathered David's town. The witnesses note how the reformed and grammatical-historical voices supplement each other here: Gill reads the town-names collectively, as standing for their inhabitants, while Keil and Delitzsch anchor each in its place and its later fame, tracing the Netophathites down to the men who returned from exile (Nehemiah 7:26). That a family of Salma's house should still be named among the returning remnant is, for the witnesses, the very point of the register: the Chronicler writes for a restored people who could find their own villages and forefathers in these lists, and know themselves the true heirs of the clans that first peopled the hills about Bethlehem, the town toward which the whole chapter, and the whole hope of Judah, has been leaning.
55:
"And the families of the scribes which dwelt at Jabez; the Tirathites, the Shimeathites, and Suchathites. These are the Kenites that came of Hemath, the father of the house of Rechab." The chapter closes on a company of scribes, and the witnesses gather its several threads. The reformed John Gill notes that these were families of scribes who dwelt at Jabez, a city in Judah, whose founder was perhaps the Jabez of 1 Chronicles 4:9, and that learned men dwelt there.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place Jabez near Zorah, and take the three family names — Tirathites, Shimeathites, Suchathites — as proper names, the descendants of men called Tira, Shimea, and Suchah. The Vulgate, they note, rendered the words as appellatives, "singing and sounding and dwelling in tents," but they judge that interpretation unproven; the names are best read as clans. Most striking is the closing note, that these scribal families were not of Judah at all by blood but "Kenites that came of Hemath, the father of the house of Rechab" — the ancestral house of the Rechabites (Jeremiah 35:2).

The wesleyan-arminian voices draw out that foreign strain. John Wesley explains that these scribes, whether civil notaries or teachers of the law, were reckoned here not as native Judahites but as Kenites who dwelt among the tribe, allied by marriage and so in a manner incorporated into it. Adam Clarke relays the Targum's soaring legend — that they were Rechabites and disciples of Jabez, sprung from the family of Moses through his father-in-law, whose "righteousness profited them more than chariots and horses" — and then gives the plain observation that crowns the chapter: "in this chapter many names of cities are given as the names of men." So the witnesses end where the whole register has tended. The genealogy of Judah gathers in the end even a clan of Kenite strangers, converted and settled and set to the sacred work of the scribes; and the sober word of Clarke, that towns have here been written as men, unlocks the chapter's method one last time — a family tree that is also a map, a roll of the people of God that has room within it for the incorporated foreigner as truly as for the sons of Judah, all bound into the tribe from which the King and the Kingdom would come.
2:
With Dan, Joseph, and Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher the roll of the twelve is completed, and the witnesses pause less on the names themselves than on the order in which they stand and the purpose they serve. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the finished list of the sons of Israel as the foundation on which the whole edifice of the following genealogies is raised; having named the patriarchs, the Chronicler will at once narrow his gaze to Judah, whose families he traces from v.3 onward, reserving the other tribes for the chapters that follow. The naming here is deliberately compressed — no mothers, no comment, only the remaining heads set in their rank.

The reformed John Gill had already drawn attention to the studied arrangement that this verse completes: the sons of the handmaids are not thrust to the end but woven in among the sons of the wives, so that between Dan and Naphtali, Bilhah's sons, stand Joseph and Benjamin, the children of Rachel. The effect is that the two youngest and best-beloved, the sons of the loved wife, are set in the heart of the list rather than at its close. Gill observes that the sons are given neither strictly by birth nor strictly by mother, but in an order of the Chronicler's own, which honours each tribe as a member of the one Israel.

Neither witness makes Dan, Gad, or Asher the occasion of doctrine; the point they press is structural, and the very brevity of the notice is itself a kind of statement. The twelve are named together, once, as the trunk from which every branch to come will spring; and then the record turns aside — as the whole book will — to follow the royal and messianic line of Judah, leaving the northern tribes to be gathered up in their season. Keil and Delitzsch note that these are the same names that stood in the roll of those who came into Egypt, so that the Chronicler, writing long after the return from exile, deliberately re-lays the ancient foundation of the nation before building his history of Judah and David upon it. Israel is one people of twelve tribes; but the history he means to tell runs through the one tribe from which the King would come.
11:
"And Nahshon begat Salma, and Salma begat Boaz." With these two links the royal genealogy passes through the very ground of the book of Ruth, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch are the witness who marks it. This descent, they observe, stands also in Ruth 4:20, where the same chain is drawn from Nahshon the prince through Salma to Boaz; and they note that the name given here as Salma appears there in the fuller forms Salmah and Salmon. So the Chronicler's terse "Salma begat Boaz" is the same event that the closing verses of Ruth record with such care, the genealogy that book was written, in part, to establish.

The weight of the verse, for the witnesses, lies in Boaz. He is the man of Bethlehem who took Ruth the Moabitess to wife and became, by the law of the kinsman-redeemer, the father of Obed and so the great-grandfather of David. Keil and Delitzsch had already warned, at v.10, that this stretch of the line is severely telescoped — four generations from Nahshon to David where more than four hundred years must be spanned, so that less famous ancestors have been silently omitted. Salma and Boaz are therefore not necessarily father and son in the strict sense, but the remembered names by which the descent is anchored. Keil and Delitzsch carry the same chain one step further in their comment, to Obed and to Jesse, so that this verse and the next lay down the four great links — Salma, Boaz, Obed, Jesse — that bring the promise from the Exodus generation to the father of David.

Yet the witnesses do not let the compression dull the wonder of the line. Through Boaz the blood of a Moabite convert entered the ancestry of Israel's king, so that the same providence which had preserved the seed of Pharez through the wilderness now gathered a Gentile woman into the royal house. The Chronicler, writing to a restored community anxious about its pedigree, sets down the plain links — Nahshon, Salma, Boaz — and lets the reader who knows the story of Ruth supply the rest: that the throne of David was built not only on the princes of Judah but on the faith of a stranger who said, "thy people shall be my people," her name written for ever into the genealogy of the coming King.
15:
"Ozem the sixth, David the seventh." The roll of Jesse's sons closes on the name for which the whole chapter exists, and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on the number. David is here the seventh, though 1 Samuel 16:10 shows Jesse had eight sons; probably, Wesley concludes, one of them died soon after that time, and so is not reckoned in this later register of the family. The genealogy counts the living heads of the house, and the son who left no continuing place drops quietly from the list.

The witnesses let the bare ordinal carry its weight. That David should stand seventh — the youngest named, the shepherd left with the sheep when Samuel came — is of a piece with the whole story of his choosing: the last and least in his father's house set at the head of God's purpose. The reformed John Gill's fuller reckoning at v.13, weighing whether the uncounted son were of another mother, or dead before David's reign, or the Elihu of 1 Chronicles 27:18, stands behind Wesley's briefer note; but the two agree that the discrepancy touches nothing essential, for the register was never meant to be an exhaustive muster of Jesse's children. Where the prophet Samuel had made Jesse pass seven sons before him and then send for the youngest from the flock (1 Samuel 16:11), the Chronicler simply sets that seventh-named son at the head of the family's memory.

What it was meant to do is reach David, and here it does. From the twelve sons of Israel, through the sceptre-tribe of Judah, down the long telescoped descent of Pharez and Hezron and Ram and Boaz, the line arrives at the seventh son of a Bethlehemite farmer — the rod that should come forth out of the stem of Jesse. The Chronicler has spent an entire chapter of names to set this one name in its place, and having reached it he pauses only to note David's sisters and their sons before the royal house proper is unfolded. The witnesses see the sober glory of it: the promise made to Judah has found its man, and the genealogy of grace has done its work in bringing the reader to David.
